content.text:
<timestamp>Thursday, May 28, 2026, 12:44 PM (UTC+5:30)</timestamp>
<user_query>
Build a Node.js Express application called backtrace-target. This app is a deliberate test target for an incident-response agent that watches CloudWatch logs, classifies errors, and opens fix PRs. The app must look like a realistic small e-commerce checkout service and must break in predictable, classifiable ways when specific endpoints are hit.
Repo layout to create:

├── src/
│   ├── server.js
│   ├── logger.js
│   ├── routes/
│   │   ├── healthy.js
│   │   └── failures.js
│   └── middleware/
│       └── requestContext.js
├── tests/
│   └── checkout.test.js
├── scripts/
│   └── load-gen.js
├── .github/workflows/
│   ├── ci.yml
│   └── deploy.yml
├── Dockerfile
├── .dockerignore
├── .gitignore
├── package.json
└── README.md
Runtime + dependencies: Node.js 20, Express 4, pino for JSON logging, nanoid for request IDs, supertest + jest for tests. No TypeScript — plain JS, ES modules.
Logger (src/logger.js): Use pino configured to output single-line JSON to stdout. Every log line must include: timestamp (ISO 8601), level (INFO / WARN / ERROR), service ("backtrace-target"), commitSha (read from process.env.COMMIT_SHA, default "unknown"), requestId, endpoint, message. For ERROR-level logs also include exceptionType and stack. Export a createLogger(requestId, endpoint) helper that returns a child logger with those fields bound.
Request context middleware (src/middleware/requestContext.js): Generate a request ID like req_<nanoid 10 chars> if no x-request-id header is present, otherwise reuse the incoming one. Attach the request ID and a child logger to req. Log a request.start INFO line on entry and a request.end INFO line on completion with duration in ms and status code.
Healthy route (src/routes/healthy.js): POST /checkout accepts { userId, amount, currency }. Log lifecycle breadcrumbs at INFO level: checkout.start, payment.processing, stripe.call.start, stripe.call.success, payment.success. Return { orderId, status: "confirmed" }. Include a GET /healthz returning { ok: true }.
Failure routes (src/routes/failures.js): Four endpoints, each producing a distinct, classifiable failure:

POST /fail/code-bug — Simulate a payment bug. Log lifecycle up to payment.processing, then call a function processPayment(order) that attempts order.customer.email.toLowerCase() where customer is undefined. The resulting TypeError must bubble up with a stack trace that clearly references src/routes/failures.js. Catch it in an error handler and log at ERROR level with full stack.
POST /fail/dependency — Simulate an upstream service failure. Log stripe.call.start, then throw an error with message "connect ECONNREFUSED 54.231.0.1:443" and code: "ECONNREFUSED". The log must clearly indicate an external dependency failure (include dependency: "stripe" field).
POST /fail/saturation — Simulate traffic-induced saturation. Block the event loop with a CPU-busy loop for 3 seconds, log a request.slow WARN line, then return HTTP 503 with { error: "service overloaded" }. ERROR log must include reason: "saturation" and cpuLoadMs field.
POST /fail/random — Randomly invoke one of the above three handlers.

Top-level error handler in server.js: Catches any thrown error from routes, logs at ERROR level with exceptionType (the error's constructor name), message, and stack, then returns HTTP 500 with { error: "internal error", requestId }. Crucially, the JSON log must look like this shape:
json{
  "timestamp": "2026-05-28T14:03:12.456Z",
  "level": "ERROR",
  "service": "backtrace-target",
  "commitSha": "a3f9c21",
  "requestId": "req_a3f9c21b8e",
  "endpoint": "POST /fail/code-bug",
  "exceptionType": "TypeError",
  "message": "Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'toLowerCase')",
  "stack": "TypeError: ...\n    at processPayment (/app/src/routes/failures.js:42:18)\n    ..."
}
src/server.js: Wire middleware in this order — express.json(), requestContext, routes, then the error handler. Listen on process.env.PORT || 3000. On boot, log an INFO line server.start including commitSha and port.
Tests (tests/checkout.test.js): Using jest + supertest, test that POST /checkout with a valid body returns 200 and a confirmed status, and that GET /healthz returns 200. Keep tests minimal but real — they need to actually pass so the agent's Phase 7 has a meaningful signal.
Load generator (scripts/load-gen.js): Standalone Node script that takes a --url flag (the deployed endpoint) and a --mode flag with values steady (95% healthy + 5% mixed failures, runs indefinitely at ~2 req/sec) or burst-<failure-type> (e.g. burst-code-bug, hits that failure 20 times in 10 seconds to trigger the agent's "rate above baseline" detection). Uses only built-in node:fetch, no extra deps. Logs each request's status to stdout.
Dockerfile: Multi-stage. Stage 1 installs deps with npm ci. Stage 2 is a slim runtime (node:20-alpine) that copies only node_modules, src/, and package.json. Accept ARG COMMIT_SHA and set ENV COMMIT_SHA=$COMMIT_SHA. Expose 3000. CMD ["node", "src/server.js"]. Run as non-root user.
.dockerignore: Exclude node_modules, .git, tests, scripts, .github, README.md.
.github/workflows/ci.yml: Triggers on pull_request and workflow_dispatch. Sets up Node 20, runs npm ci and npm test.
.github/workflows/deploy.yml: Triggers on push to main. Steps:

Checkout code.
Configure AWS credentials via OIDC (use aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4, role-to-assume is ${{ secrets.AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }}, region is ${{ secrets.AWS_REGION }}).
Log in to ECR.
Build Docker image with --build-arg COMMIT_SHA=${{ github.sha }}, tag it with both ${{ github.sha }} and latest, push both tags to ECR (repo name from ${{ secrets.ECR_REPOSITORY }}).
Render new task definition with the new image tag (use aws-actions/amazon-ecs-render-task-definition@v1).
Deploy to ECS (use aws-actions/amazon-ecs-deploy-task-definition@v2), service name from ${{ secrets.ECS_SERVICE }}, cluster from ${{ secrets.ECS_CLUSTER }}, wait for service stability.

Include the task definition file at infra/task-definition.json with placeholders for image URI, task role ARN, execution role ARN, and CloudWatch log group — the GitHub Action will fill in the image at deploy time. Container port 3000, awslogs driver pointing to /ecs/backtrace-target, log stream prefix ecs.
package.json scripts: start runs node src/server.js, dev runs with --watch, test runs jest, loadgen runs node scripts/load-gen.js.
README.md: Document local dev (npm install, npm start, then curl localhost:3000/fail/code-bug), the four failure endpoints with their classifications, and how to run the load generator. Do NOT include AWS deployment steps in the README — those live elsewhere.
Important constraints:

Keep all source files under ~150 lines each. Readability matters more than cleverness.
The failure endpoints' stack traces must be authentic — actually throw real errors, don't fabricate stack strings.
Logs must be single-line JSON, never multi-line, never pretty-printed.
No external services beyond CloudWatch (no Sentry, no Datadog SDK, etc).
Commit messages should be conventional commits style.
</user_query>

Creating the full `backtrace-target` scaffold now with Express routes, logging, middleware, tests, CI/deploy workflows, Docker assets, and docs, then I’ll run tests to verify everything passes end-to-end.

content.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: E:/Trilogy/Backtrace_target_TI/.github/workflows/deploy.yml
+name: Deploy
+
+on:
+  push:
+    branches:
+      - main
+
+jobs:
+  deploy:
+    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
+    permissions:
+      id-token: write
+      contents: read
+    env:
+      AWS_REGION: ${{ secrets.AWS_REGION }}
+      ECR_REPOSITORY: ${{ secrets.ECR_REPOSITORY }}
+    steps:
+      - name: Checkout
+        uses: actions/checkout@v4
+
+      - name: Configure AWS credentials
+        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
+        with:
+          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }}
+          aws-region: ${{ secrets.AWS_REGION }}
+
+      - name: Log in to ECR
+        id: login-ecr
+        uses: aws-actions/amazon-ecr-login@v2
+
+      - name: Build and push image
+        env:
+          ECR_REGISTRY: ${{ steps.login-ecr.outputs.registry }}
+          IMAGE_SHA: ${{ github.sha }}
+        run: |
+          IMAGE_URI="$ECR_REGISTRY/$ECR_REPOSITORY"
+          docker build --build-arg COMMIT_SHA="$IMAGE_SHA" -t "$IMAGE_URI:$IMAGE_SHA" -t "$IMAGE_URI:latest" .
+          docker push "$IMAGE_URI:$IMAGE_SHA"
+          docker push "$IMAGE_URI:latest"
+          echo "image=$IMAGE_URI:$IMAGE_SHA" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
+
+      - name: Render ECS task definition
+        id: task-def
+        uses: aws-actions/amazon-ecs-render-task-definition@v1
+        with:
+          task-definition: infra/task-definition.json
+          container-name: backtrace-target
+          image: ${{ steps.build-and-push.outputs.image }}
+
+      - name: Deploy task definition
+        uses: aws-actions/amazon-ecs-deploy-task-definition@v2
+        with:
+          task-definition: ${{ steps.task-def.outputs.task-definition }}
+          service: ${{ secrets.ECS_SERVICE }}
+          cluster: ${{ secrets.ECS_CLUSTER }}
+          wait-for-service-stability: true
*** End Patch

+FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
+WORKDIR /app
+COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
+RUN npm ci --omit=dev
+
+FROM node:20-alpine AS runtime
+WORKDIR /app
+
+ARG COMMIT_SHA=unknown
+ENV COMMIT_SHA=$COMMIT_SHA
+ENV NODE_ENV=production
+
+COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
+COPY --chown=node:node package.json ./package.json
+COPY --chown=node:node src ./src
+
+EXPOSE 3000
+USER node
+CMD ["node", "src/server.js"]
*** End Patch

content.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: E:/Trilogy/Backtrace_target_TI/README.md
+# backtrace-target
+
+`backtrace-target` is a small Express checkout service designed to intentionally fail in predictable ways for incident-response testing.
+
+## Local development
+
+1. Install dependencies:
+   - `npm install`
+2. Start the service:
+   - `npm start`
+3. Trigger a known failure:
+   - `curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/fail/code-bug -H "content-type: application/json" -d "{}"`
+
+The service runs on `http://localhost:3000` by default.
+
+## Endpoints
+
+### Healthy endpoints
+
+- `GET /healthz` - returns `{ "ok": true }`.
+- `POST /checkout` - accepts `{ userId, amount, currency }` and returns a confirmed order.
+
+### Failure endpoints and classifications
+
+- `POST /fail/code-bug` - throws a real `TypeError` from application code (`src/routes/failures.js`) to simulate a code defect.
+- `POST /fail/dependency` - throws `ECONNREFUSED` for a mocked Stripe call, representing an external dependency outage.
+- `POST /fail/saturation` - blocks CPU for ~3 seconds, logs a slow request warning and saturation error, then returns `503`.
+- `POST /fail/random` - randomly executes one of the three failure scenarios above.
+
+## Load generator
+
+Run all commands from the repository root.
+
+- Steady traffic (~2 req/sec, mostly healthy):
+  - `npm run loadgen -- --url http://localhost:3000 --mode steady`
+- Burst traffic for one failure type (20 requests in ~10 seconds):
+  - `npm run loadgen -- --url http://localhost:3000 --mode burst-code-bug`
+  - `npm run loadgen -- --url http://localhost:3000 --mode burst-dependency`
+  - `npm run loadgen -- --url http://localhost:3000 --mode burst-saturation`
*** End Patch

content.text:
Implemented the full `backtrace-target` Node 20 + Express 4 project in `E:/Trilogy/Backtrace_target_TI` with the requested structure, failure behaviors, logging model, test suite, load generator, Docker setup, ECS task definition, and GitHub workflows.

### What I added

- App runtime and dependencies in `package.json` (ES modules, Express, pino, nanoid, jest, supertest) plus scripts:
  - `start`, `dev`, `test`, `loadgen`
- Request-scoped JSON logging:
  - `src/logger.js`
  - `src/middleware/requestContext.js`
- Routes:
  - `src/routes/healthy.js` (`POST /checkout`, `GET /healthz`)
  - `src/routes/failures.js` (`/fail/code-bug`, `/fail/dependency`, `/fail/saturation`, `/fail/random`)
- Server wiring and top-level error handler:
  - `src/server.js` (middleware order matches your requirement)
- Tests:
  - `tests/checkout.test.js`
- Load generator:
  - `scripts/load-gen.js` with `steady` and `burst-*` modes
- Container/deploy assets:
  - `Dockerfile` (multi-stage, non-root, COMMIT_SHA arg/env)
  - `.dockerignore`
  - `.gitignore`
  - `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
  - `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`
  - `infra/task-definition.json`
- Docs:
  - `README.md` (local dev + endpoint classifications + loadgen usage, no AWS deploy steps)

### Verification done

- Installed dependencies (`npm install`) and generated lockfile.
- Ran tests successfully:
  - `npm test` → 2/2 passing
- Checked diagnostics:
  - no linter errors found

### Notes on the requested behavior

- Logs are single-line JSON via `pino` to stdout.
- Request logs include `timestamp`, `level`, `service`, `commitSha`, `requestId`, `endpoint`, `message`.
- Error logs include `exceptionType` and `stack`.
- `POST /fail/code-bug` throws a real `TypeError` from `src/routes/failures.js`, so stack traces are authentic and classifiable.
- `POST /fail/dependency` throws `ECONNREFUSED` and includes dependency context (`stripe`).
- `POST /fail/saturation` busy-loops ~3s, logs `request.slow` at WARN, logs ERROR with `reason: "saturation"` + `cpuLoadMs`, and returns `503`.

If you want, I can now run a quick local smoke pass with `curl` commands against each failure endpoint and summarize the exact emitted log signatures per endpoint for your incident-response classifier.

<timestamp>Thursday, May 28, 2026, 12:53 PM (UTC+5:30)</timestamp>
<user_query>
got it but now I want it test manually so give me what all I can do in this project and also how to start this project 
</user_query>

### Start the project locally

From `e:\Trilogy\Backtrace_target_TI`:

1. Install deps:
   - `npm install`
2. Start server:
   - `npm start`
3. Service will run on:
   - `http://localhost:3000`

---

### What you can manually test

### 1) Health check
```bash
curl http://localhost:3000/healthz
```
Expected: `200` + `{"ok":true}`

### 2) Happy checkout flow
```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/checkout ^
  -H "content-type: application/json" ^
  -d "{\"userId\":\"usr_1\",\"amount\":1999,\"currency\":\"USD\"}"
```
Expected: `200` + `{ "orderId": "...", "status": "confirmed" }`

### 3) Code bug failure (real TypeError stack)
```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/fail/code-bug -H "content-type: application/json" -d "{}"
```
Expected: `500` + `{"error":"internal error","requestId":"..."}`  
Logs: `ERROR`, `exceptionType: "TypeError"`, stack pointing to `src/routes/failures.js`.

### 4) Dependency failure (Stripe ECONNREFUSED)
```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/fail/dependency -H "content-type: application/json" -d "{}"
```
Expected: `500`  
Logs: `ERROR` with message like `connect ECONNREFUSED...`, code `ECONNREFUSED`, dependency `stripe`.

### 5) Saturation failure (CPU busy loop)
```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/fail/saturation -H "content-type: application/json" -d "{}"
```
Expected: ~3s delay, then `503` + `{"error":"service overloaded"}`  
Logs: `WARN request.slow` and `ERROR` with `reason: "saturation"` + `cpuLoadMs`.

### 6) Random failure endpoint
```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/fail/random -H "content-type: application/json" -d "{}"
```
Expected: random one of the 3 failure behaviors.

---

### Useful project commands

- Run tests:
  - `npm test`
- Start in watch/dev mode:
  - `npm run dev`
- Run load generator (steady traffic):
  - `npm run loadgen -- --url http://localhost:3000 --mode steady`
- Run burst for a specific failure:
  - `npm run loadgen -- --url http://localhost:3000 --mode burst-code-bug`
  - `npm run loadgen -- --url http://localhost:3000 --mode burst-dependency`
  - `npm run loadgen -- --url http://localhost:3000 --mode burst-saturation`

---

### Tip for manual log tracing

Pass your own request ID so you can track one request across logs:

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/checkout ^
  -H "content-type: application/json" ^
  -H "x-request-id: req_manual_001" ^
  -d "{\"userId\":\"usr_1\",\"amount\":1999,\"currency\":\"USD\"}"
```

If you want, I can also give you a **10-minute manual test checklist** (exact sequence + expected status/log signature per step).

<timestamp>Thursday, May 28, 2026, 1:05 PM (UTC+5:30)</timestamp>
<user_query>
docker build -t backtrace_target_TI:local --build-arg COMMIT_SHA=test123 .
[+] Building 0.0s (0/0)                              docker:desktop-linux
ERROR: failed to build: invalid tag "backtrace_target_TI:local": repository name must be lowercase

look at this error and resolve it
</user_query>
That error is from Docker image naming rules (repository must be lowercase). I’ll validate the corrected build command in this repo and give you the exact working command(s).

input.description: Run Docker build with lowercase tag

content.text:
The build error is because Docker image repository names must be lowercase.  
Your tag had uppercase letters: `backtrace_target_TI:local` (`TI` breaks it).

Use this instead:

```bash
docker build -t backtrace-target-ti:local --build-arg COMMIT_SHA=test123 .
```

I ran that exact command in your project, and it built successfully.

If you want to run it too:

```bash
docker run --rm -p 3000:3000 backtrace-target-ti:local
```

Then test:

```bash
curl http://localhost:3000/healthz
```

input.description: Verify npm loadgen argument parsing works
Test warning-free loadgen invocation
Test positional arguments through npm run

content.text:
You found a real issue: on your npm/PowerShell setup, `--url` and `--mode` are being eaten by npm, so the script got only positional values.

I resolved it by updating `scripts/load-gen.js` to support:
- `--url ... --mode ...`
- `--url=... --mode=...`
- positional fallback: `<url> <mode>`

### Use this command now (works cleanly)
```bash
npm run loadgen -- http://localhost:3000 burst-code-bug
```

I verified it runs and sends 20 requests, returning `500` responses as expected for `code-bug`.

### Alternative (also works)
```bash
node scripts/load-gen.js --url http://localhost:3000 --mode burst-code-bug
```

If you want, I can also patch `README.md` so it documents the Windows-safe command format explicitly.

<timestamp>Thursday, May 28, 2026, 3:10 PM (UTC+5:30)</timestamp>
<user_query>
In the existing project, make the following changes. Do not touch unrelated files.
1. Modify src/logger.js to write logs to both stdout and a local file.
Use pino.multistream to fan out to two destinations:

process.stdout (existing behavior — keep this)
A file at ./logs/app.jsonl

The log file path should be configurable via an environment variable LOG_FILE_PATH, defaulting to ./logs/app.jsonl. The directory must be created if it doesn't exist (use fs.mkdirSync with recursive: true at the top of the file, synchronously, before pino initializes). Both streams must receive the same single-line JSON output. The output format must not change — every existing field (timestamp, level, service, commitSha, requestId, endpoint, message, event, exceptionType, stack, etc.) must still appear in both stdout and the file, identically.
Make sure file writes are flushed promptly. If using pino.destination(), configure it with sync: false for performance but make sure process.on('exit') and process.on('SIGINT') handlers in src/server.js call .flushSync() on the file stream before exit so no log lines are lost on Ctrl+C.
2. Update .gitignore to include:
logs/
*.jsonl
3. Add a deployments.json file at the repo root with this initial content:
json{
  "deployments": []
}
This file simulates the deployment history that would normally come from ECS task definitions + GitHub Actions. The agent will read from it later. Do not gitignore this file — it should be tracked.
4. Add a scripts/record-deployment.js script that appends a new deployment entry to deployments.json. It should:

Read the current Git commit SHA via git rev-parse HEAD
Read the current Git commit timestamp via git show -s --format=%cI HEAD
Append { "commitSha": "<sha>", "deployedAt": "<iso-timestamp>", "filesChanged": [...] } to the deployments array in deployments.json
For filesChanged, use git diff-tree --no-commit-id --name-only -r HEAD to get the list of files in that commit
Pretty-print the JSON output (2-space indent)
Use only Node built-ins (child_process.execSync, fs) — no extra dependencies

Add an npm script record-deploy in package.json that runs node scripts/record-deployment.js.
The intent: every time you "deploy" locally (which for V1 just means making a commit), you'll run npm run record-deploy to log it. This gives the agent a real deployment timeline to correlate errors against in Phase 3, without needing AWS.
5. Update README.md to add a short new section titled "Log file output and deployment recording" explaining:

Logs are written to ./logs/app.jsonl in addition to stdout
The file is JSON-lines format (one JSON object per line)
To record a deployment, run npm run record-deploy after committing
deployments.json tracks the deployment history that the agent uses for suspect commit correlation

6. Verify nothing else breaks. After the changes:

npm start must still work and produce identical stdout output to before
npm test must still pass
The load generator must still work unchanged
After running the server and hitting /fail/code-bug once, ./logs/app.jsonl must exist and contain the same JSON log lines that appeared in stdout

Do not add new dependencies. Do not change the existing log schema. Do not modify the failure routes or the request context middleware. Do not introduce TypeScript. Keep all changes minimal and focused.
</user_query>

I’ll make focused edits to `logger`, `server`, `package.json`, `.gitignore`, `README.md`, and add the two new files, then run start/test/loadgen checks plus a `/fail/code-bug` hit to confirm the file log output.

I’ve got the target files loaded; next I’m patching `logger` + `server` for dual-stream logging with safe flush-on-exit, then I’ll add deployment recording and run the verification commands you requested.

content.text:
Implemented exactly the requested, focused changes and verified behavior.

### What I changed

- Updated `src/logger.js` to fan out logs with `pino.multistream` to:
  - `process.stdout`
  - file stream at `LOG_FILE_PATH` or default `./logs/app.jsonl`
- Added synchronous directory creation before logger init:
  - `fs.mkdirSync(path.dirname(resolvedLogFilePath), { recursive: true })`
- Kept log schema/format unchanged (same single-line JSON, same fields).
- Exported `logFileStream` from `src/logger.js`.

- Updated `src/server.js` to flush file logs on shutdown:
  - `process.on("exit", ...)` calls `logFileStream.flushSync()`
  - `process.on("SIGINT", ...)` calls `logFileStream.flushSync()` then exits

- Updated `.gitignore`:
  - `logs/`
  - `*.jsonl`

- Added tracked `deployments.json` at repo root with:
  - `{ "deployments": [] }`

- Added `scripts/record-deployment.js`:
  - Uses only Node built-ins (`child_process.execSync`, `fs`)
  - Reads `HEAD` SHA and commit timestamp
  - Reads changed files from `git diff-tree --no-commit-id --name-only -r HEAD`
  - Appends `{ commitSha, deployedAt, filesChanged }` to `deployments.json`
  - Pretty-prints with 2-space indent

- Updated `package.json` scripts:
  - added `"record-deploy": "node scripts/record-deployment.js"`

- Updated `README.md` with section:
  - **Log file output and deployment recording**

---

### Verification results

- `npm test` passes (2/2 tests).
- `npm start` code path is healthy; local check on default `3000` was blocked by an already-occupied port on your machine, so I verified by running on `PORT=3100`.
- Hitting `POST /fail/code-bug` created `./logs/app.jsonl`.
- The file contains the same JSON log lines as stdout (same entries/fields).
- Load generator still works:
  - `npm run loadgen -- http://localhost:3100 burst-code-bug` succeeded.

If you want, I can now also add a tiny `npm run start:3100` helper script just for local conflict-free checks (without changing existing `start`).

<timestamp>Thursday, May 28, 2026, 3:15 PM (UTC+5:30)</timestamp>

No follow-up action is needed. If you want to run it again cleanly:

- `cmd /c "set PORT=3100&& npm start"`  
- then stop with `Ctrl+C` for a graceful exit/flush.

content.text:
The app is running in Docker on **http://localhost:3000**.

I had to fix one Docker issue first: the container was crashing because the `node` user could not create `/app/logs`. I updated the `Dockerfile` to pre-create that directory with the right permissions, then rebuilt and started the container.

### Commands used

```bash
docker build -t backtrace-target-ti:local --build-arg COMMIT_SHA=local .
docker run -d --name backtrace-target -p 3000:3000 backtrace-target-ti:local
```

### Quick checks

```bash
curl http://localhost:3000/healthz
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/fail/code-bug -H "content-type: application/json" -d "{}"
```

### Useful Docker commands

- View logs: `docker logs -f backtrace-target`
- Stop: `docker stop backtrace-target`
- Remove: `docker rm -f backtrace-target`
- Restart after code changes: rebuild image, then run again

### Note

If `npm start` is also running locally on port 3000, stop it first or Docker will fail with a port conflict. Right now the container owns port 3000.

If you want logs on your host machine (not only inside the container), run:

```bash
docker run -d --name backtrace-target -p 3000:3000 -v "%cd%/logs:/app/logs" backtrace-target-ti:local
```

input.new_string:
## Log file output and deployment recording

- Logs are written to `./logs/app.jsonl` in addition to stdout.
- The log file is JSON-lines format (one JSON object per line).
- After committing, run `npm run record-deploy` to record a deployment event.
- `deployments.json` stores deployment history that the agent uses for suspect commit correlation.

When running in Docker, mount the logs directory so entries appear on your host machine:

```bash
docker compose up -d --build
```

Or with plain Docker:

```bash
docker run -d --name backtrace-target -p 3000:3000 -v "%cd%/logs:/app/logs" backtrace-target-ti:local
```

Without the volume mount, logs are written inside the container at `/app/logs/app.jsonl` only.

input.description: Restart container with logs volume mount

content.text: [REDACTED]

input.command: curl.exe -s -X POST http://localhost:3000/fail/code-bug -H "content-type: application/json" -d "{}"; Start-Sleep -Seconds 1; Get-Content logs/app.jsonl -Tail 3
input.description: Trigger failure and verify host log file

content.text:
The logs **were being written** — just not to the file you were looking at.

### Why it happened

You're running the app in **Docker**. Without a volume mount, logs go to:

- **Inside container:** `/app/logs/app.jsonl` ✅ (your `req_HoiCIuLmUi` and `req_A8THr7wG4s` are there)
- **On your machine:** `./logs/app.jsonl` ❌ (only old entries from earlier `npm start` runs)

Docker keeps its own filesystem separate from your project folder unless you explicitly mount a directory.

### What I fixed

1. Added `docker-compose.yml` with a logs volume mount:
   - `./logs:/app/logs`
2. Restarted the container with:
   - `docker compose up -d --build`
3. Verified: after hitting `/fail/code-bug`, new lines now appear in your host `./logs/app.jsonl`.

### How to run going forward

Use Docker Compose (recommended):

```bash
docker compose up -d --build
```

Or plain Docker with volume:

```bash
docker run -d --name backtrace-target -p 3000:3000 -v "%cd%/logs:/app/logs" backtrace-target-ti:local
```

### Quick test

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/fail/code-bug -H "content-type: application/json" -d "{}"
```

Then open `logs/app.jsonl` — you should see new JSON lines with the matching `requestId`.

**Note:** Your earlier Docker requests (`req_HoiCIuLmUi`, `req_A8THr7wG4s`) are still only inside the old container's internal log file, not on the host. New requests after the volume mount will show up in `./logs/app.jsonl`.

[REDACTED]

Those background checks confirmed the root cause: your `req_HoiCIuLmUi` and `req_A8THr7wG4s` requests **did** produce logs, but they were written inside the Docker container (`/app/logs/app.jsonl` and `docker logs`), not to your host `./logs/app.jsonl`.

That’s already fixed by restarting with the volume mount via `docker compose up -d --build`. New requests should now appear in your local `logs/app.jsonl` file.
